title: BROWT
text:
Abstract
This report documents the design and evaluation of an agent that plays Brawl Stars using only what is on the screen — no access to the game’s internal state. A frame is captured, objects are detected, the detections are turned into a state, and a reinforcement-learning policy chooses an action that is sent back to the game. The change in the resulting frames is the reward.
The aim of these pages is scientific validation: to expose the results obtained at each stage and the method used to obtain them, rather than to teach how to operate the tool.

title: BROWT
text:
Task studied: Solo Showdown (MVP scope).
Approach: perception (YOLOv11) → state → reinforcement learning, closed in a perception-action loop.
Status: the perception stage is under evaluation; later stages are work in progress and will be reported as results arrive.

title: BROWT
text:
Hypothesis
A purely vision-based agent, with no privileged access to game internals, can learn a useful Solo Showdown policy if the perception stage produces a state that is accurate and stable enough for a reinforcement-learning policy to act on.

title: BROWT
text:
Method in one diagram





flowchart LR
    F[Frame] -->|detection -> state| G[RL policy]
    G -->|action| H[Game]
    H --> F
    H -.reward.-> G






Each stage is studied on its own tab: how it was built (method) and what it achieves (results). The Architecture tab gives the full picture; the per-stage tabs report the evidence.

title: BROWT
text:
How to read this report
The tabs follow the perception-to-action pipeline. Stages still being evaluated are marked work in progress and will be filled in as their results become available.

title: Architecture
text:
The pipeline under study
The agent is organised along three stages that turn a frame into an action. Each stage is evaluated on its own tab.
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIM[1. Capture]
        A[Emulator] --> B[Screen capture]
    end
    subgraph DET[2. Perception]
        C[Object detection<br/>YOLOv11] --> D[State restructuring]
    end
    subgraph AI[3. Decision]
        E[RL policy]
    end

    B -->|frame| C
    D -->|state| E
At run time the stages form a closed perception-action loop: each action changes the game, producing the next frame, and the change in frames is the reward signal that trains the policy.
flowchart LR
    F[Frame] -->|detection -> state| G[RL policy]
    G -->|action: direction + shoot| H[Action in game]
    H --> I[New frame]
    I -->|reward signal| G
    I --> F

title: Architecture
text:
Stages

Capture. The game runs in an emulator at a fixed resolution; frames are grabbed from the window. See Capture.
Perception. A YOLOv11 detector returns bounding boxes, which are restructured into a state vector. See Detection and State.
Decision. A reinforcement-learning policy maps the state to an action (movement direction + shoot), injected back into the game. See RL and Inputs.

title: Architecture
text:
Materials







Stage
Method / tooling




Capture
LDPlayer 9 emulator, frames via mss


Perception
YOLOv11 (Ultralytics), trained on the Roboflow bloxxy/brawl-stars-dataset (10 classes, MIT)


Decision
reinforcement learning (Q-learning / PPO, to be compared)


Inputs
ADB (swipe for movement, tap to shoot)

title: Architecture
text:
Why vision-only
The agent is deliberately restricted to pixels, with no privileged access to game internals. This keeps the result a fair test of the central hypothesis — that perception accurate and stable enough to support a learned policy can be recovered from the screen alone.

title: Roadmap
text:
Open questions

Static terrain. The detector recovers mobile entities but not bushes or walls. Whether static map layout can be sourced per map, rather than detected from pixels, is open (see Capture).
Ban risk. Emulator and automated play may be flagged by Supercell. Tracked as a risk, not yet mitigated (see Inputs).
